title: Jessica Harcourt

infobox.value: Jessica Edna Harcourt  (1905-04-07)7 April 1905  [Woollahra, Sydney](/wiki/Woollahra,_Sydney "Woollahra, Sydney"), Australia.

(1905–1988) was an Australian [mannequin](/wiki/Model_(person) "Model (person)"), author and actress, best known for playing a leading role in *[For the Term of His Natural Life](/wiki/For_the_Term_of_His_Natural_Life_(1927_film) "For the Term of His Natural Life (1927 film)")* (1927).

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Australian actress (1905–1988)

| Jessica Harcourt | |
| --- | --- |
| "The Maid and the Magnolia", Harcourt in 1937 | |
| Born | Jessica Edna Harcourt  (1905-04-07)7 April 1905  [Woollahra, Sydney](/wiki/Woollahra,_Sydney "Woollahra, Sydney"), Australia. |
| Died | 23 August 1988(1988-08-23) (aged 83)  [Ipswich, Queensland](/wiki/Ipswich,_Queensland "Ipswich, Queensland"), Australia |
| Occupations | [Mannequin](/wiki/Model_(person) "Model (person)"), author, actress |
| Spouse | F. J. Thwaites  ​  ​ (m. 1938; died 1979)​ |

**Jessica Harcourt** (1905–1988) was an Australian [mannequin](/wiki/Model_(person) "Model (person)"), author and actress, best known for playing a leading role in *[For the Term of His Natural Life](/wiki/For_the_Term_of_His_Natural_Life_(1927_film) "For the Term of His Natural Life (1927 film)")* (1927).

## Biography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jessica_Harcourt&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Biography")]

Jessica Edna Harcourt was born on 7 April 1905 in Woollahra, Sydney. She was the daughter of Aubrey William Harcourt, a mercer, and Ada Hobson. Harcourt believed her family were direct descendants of Ivo d'Harcourt, a companion of William the Conqueror.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:2-2) As a young woman working as a programme seller at [Her Majesty's Theatre, Sydney](/wiki/Her_Majesty%27s_Theatre,_Sydney "Her Majesty's Theatre, Sydney"), she first caught the eye of [Sir John Longstaff](/wiki/John_Longstaff "John Longstaff"),[[3]](#cite_note-3) and then her beauty attracted the attention of George Highland,[[4]](#cite_note-4) a producer of [J. C. Williamson Ltd](/wiki/J._C._Williamson_Ltd "J. C. Williamson Ltd"). She became a chorus girl, appearing in *[Whirled into Happiness](/wiki/Whirled_into_Happiness "Whirled into Happiness")* in 1924. She was quickly dubbed "Australia's loveliest girl".[[5]](#cite_note-5) Longstaff described her as “more English than Australian, her lovely coloring and features, combined with character and high intelligence, give her very high rank among the many beautiful women I have seen.”[[6]](#cite_note-6) Harcourt recognised the opportunity of advancing her career and undertook to do intensive elocution lessons, saying "I don't care how hard I have to work so long as I amount to something in the end."[[7]](#cite_note-7) Harcourt received requests for her photo from international newspapers so they could place her face alongside their own beauties.[[8]](#cite_note-8) Harcourt started modelling and not long after arriving in Melbourne, was already working as a mannequin for [Buckley and Nunn](/wiki/Buckley_%26_Nunn "Buckley & Nunn").[[9]](#cite_note-9)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/Jessica_Harcourt_1924-2.png/250px-Jessica_Harcourt_1924-2.png)](/wiki/File:Jessica_Harcourt_1924-2.png)

Jessica Harcourt in 1924

Harcourt made her cinema debut as Sara Purfoy in *[For the Term of His Natural Life](/wiki/For_the_Term_of_His_Natural_Life_(1927_film) "For the Term of His Natural Life (1927 film)").* Filming commenced in 1926 and locations included [Port Arthur](/wiki/Port_Arthur,_Tasmania "Port Arthur, Tasmania") and Launceston, Tasmania and Master Pictures' studio in Bondi, NSW.[[10]](#cite_note-10) Of her performance, the director Norman Dawn said Harcourt had "acted capitally" in an emotional scene and "her film prospects were good".[[11]](#cite_note-11)

In an interview with the *Australian Women's Weekly*, Harcourt recalled her surprise at being cast as Sarah Purfoy. She said "...when it was offered to me, I was astounded. I still am! Most of the actresses in Sydney wanted it and there I was, 19, with no acting experience, and I got it!"[[12]](#cite_note-:1-12)

Not long after completing filming, Harcourt was off with Norman Dawn to Suva, Fiji to film an adaption of [Beatrice Grimshaw](/wiki/Beatrice_Grimshaw "Beatrice Grimshaw")'s novel *Conn of the Coral Seas*, a tropical comedy/drama.[[13]](#cite_note-13) The filming on location was to include "500 war canoes and outriggers and 2000 native warriors dancing in full war dress with clubs and spears".[[14]](#cite_note-14) Filming was to take six weeks and Harcourt made it back to Australia in time for the premiere of her first movie *For the Term of His Natural Life*. The film was released as *[The Adorable Outcast](/wiki/The_Adorable_Outcast "The Adorable Outcast")* and had limited box office success.

In 1928 Harcourt was cast as widow Ruby Russell in the movie *[The Russell Affair](/wiki/The_Russell_Affair "The Russell Affair")*[[15]](#cite_note-15) which was filmed in and around Sydney, including Hyde Park and the private homes of socially prominent Sydney families.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

## Portraits of Harcourt

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jessica_Harcourt&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Portraits of Harcourt")]

[Longstaff](/wiki/John_Longstaff "John Longstaff") painted several portraits of her, one of which hung in the [National Gallery](/wiki/National_Gallery "National Gallery") in London. A second portrait was exhibited at the *Twenty Melbourne Artists' Exhibition* at the [Athenaeum Hall](/wiki/Melbourne_Athenaeum "Melbourne Athenaeum") in 1925 and purchased by the [Felton Bequest](/wiki/Alfred_Felton "Alfred Felton") for the National Gallery of Victoria for 250 guineas. A miniature portrait of Harcourt, painted in watercolour on ivory by Melbourne miniaturist [Ada Whiting](/wiki/Ada_Whiting "Ada Whiting"), was selected as a finalist in the 1925 [Archibald Prize](/wiki/Archibald_Prize "Archibald Prize"). The miniature hung alongside a similar Whiting miniature of her younger sister, Rene Harcourt, who was regarded as one of the [it girls](/wiki/It_girl "It girl") of the day and who had also appeared with her sister in the stage production of *[Wildflower](/wiki/Wildflower_(musical) "Wildflower (musical)").* Whiting's portrait of Harcourt subsequently hung in the [Paris Salon](/wiki/Salon_(Paris) "Salon (Paris)") in 1934[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) and is now in the collection of the [National Gallery of Victoria](/wiki/National_Gallery_of_Victoria "National Gallery of Victoria"). Another portrait of Harcourt by Mrs E.M. Robb was exhibited at the 1932 Paris Salon[[17]](#cite_note-17) and [Ernest Buckmaster](/wiki/Ernest_Buckmaster "Ernest Buckmaster")'s portrait of Harcourt was a finalist in the 1936 Archibald Prize.[[18]](#cite_note-18) [Monte Luke](/wiki/Monte_Luke "Monte Luke"), a well-known photographer of the [Strand Arcade](/wiki/The_Strand_Arcade "The Strand Arcade"), Sydney won a silver plaque at a photographic competition in London in 1925 with one of his many photographic portraits of Harcourt.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

## Later years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jessica_Harcourt&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Later years")]

During the 1930s, Harcourt was under contract to [J. C. Williamson's](/wiki/J._C._Williamson%27s "J. C. Williamson's"), and touring Australia as a mannequin.[[20]](#cite_note-20) She produced a number of fashion shows,[[2]](#cite_note-:2-2) wrote newspaper articles,[[21]](#cite_note-21) and had a short story entitled "The Mystery Submarine" published in *[The Home](/wiki/The_Home "The Home")* magazine.[[22]](#cite_note-22) Harcourt created a range of beauty products called "Jessica Harcourt Beauty Preparations" scented with orange blossom and presented in Wedgewood blue and white containers.[[23]](#cite_note-23) In 1938 she married the Australian author [F. J. "Fred" Thwaites](/wiki/F._J._Thwaites "F. J. Thwaites") at St John's Church, Toorak and retired from acting.[[24]](#cite_note-24) She said that Thwaites did not approve of her acting, "so she devoted the rest of her time to typing manuscripts".[[12]](#cite_note-:1-12) They had two children and remained married until his death in 1979.[[12]](#cite_note-:1-12)

In 1981 she appeared at the [Sydney Film Festival](/wiki/Sydney_Film_Festival "Sydney Film Festival") for the premiere of the restoration of *[For the Term of His Natural Life](/wiki/For_the_Term_of_His_Natural_Life_(1927_film) "For the Term of His Natural Life (1927 film)")*.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

## Theatre credits

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jessica_Harcourt&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Theatre credits")]

* *Whirled into Happiness*, 1924, His Majesty's Theatre, Melbourne[[26]](#cite_note-26)
* *A Night Out,* 1924
* *Wildflower*, 1925. His Majesty's Theatre, Melbourne[[27]](#cite_note-27)
* *The Vagabond King*, 1928
* *Hello Paris*, 1930
* *Rio Rita*, 1930[[28]](#cite_note-28)

## Film credits

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jessica_Harcourt&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Film credits")]

Harcourt was regarded as a "silent screen star" as she only ever appeared in silent movies.[[12]](#cite_note-:1-12)

* *[For the Term of His Natural Life](/wiki/For_the_Term_of_His_Natural_Life_(1927_film) "For the Term of His Natural Life (1927 film)")* (1927)
* *[The Adorable Outcast](/wiki/The_Adorable_Outcast "The Adorable Outcast")* (1928)
* *[The Russell Affair](/wiki/The_Russell_Affair "The Russell Affair")* (1929)
